--- SOURCE 1: Application of self-sensing concrete sensors for bridge monitoring ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X

SUMMARY:
Application of self-sensing concrete sensors for bridge monitoring − A review of recent developments, challenges, and future prospects - ScienceDirect The application of self-sensing concrete sensors (SSCSs) in infrastructure represents a promising advancement in structural health monitoring (SHM), offering cost-effective, reliable, and sustainable long-term monitoring capabilities , . As illustrated in Table 1, various conventional sensors have been employed to monitor dynamic responses across different bridge types, including cable-stayed, concrete slab deck, steel structure, and steel arch bridges. The main contribution of this work lies in its integrated approach, which seamlessly combines detailed structural assessment (using finite element modeling rigorously validated against field measurements from the Riverside bridge case study), optimized CFRP strengthening design (based on parametric analysis applied to the Gross bridge, a 62-meter structure), and practical implementation protocols.

FULL CONTENT:
Application of self-sensing concrete sensors for bridge monitoring − A review of recent developments, challenges, and future prospects - ScienceDirect

Application of self-sensing concrete sensors for bridge monitoring − A review of recent developments, challenges, and future prospects
======================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Mohammad Siahkouhi a, Maria Rashidi a, Fidelis Mashiri a, Farhad Aslani b, Mohammad Sadegh Ayubirad c

Abstract
--------

Recent advances in self-sensing concrete technology have shown promising potential for bridge monitoring applications. The successful application of SSCSs in bridge structural health monitoring (SHM) requires careful consideration of two crucial parameters: calibration and gauge factor. These factors are essential for obtaining reliable field data and ensuring accurate monitoring outcomes. Several challenges have emerged in field applications, including environmental weathering and the need for multi-parameter calibration. To address these issues, researchers have developed various solutions, such as proactive coating of SSCSs and the use of carbon multifunctional fillers. The implementation of these sensors has been enhanced through various technological innovations, including Internet of Things (IoT) integration, hybrid sensor applications, and novel installation techniques. Digital solutions such as bridge information modelling (BrIM) and digital twin technology. The combination of SSCSs with advanced digital technologies presents a compelling case for their adoption in bridge monitoring and decision-making systems, potentially replacing conventional sensors.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0263224124024503)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0263224124024072)

Keywords
--------

Self-sensing concrete sensors

Bridge

Infrastructure

Structural health monitoring (SHM)

1. Introduction
---------------

The application of self-sensing concrete sensors (SSCSs) in infrastructure represents a promising advancement in structural health monitoring (SHM), offering cost-effective, reliable, and sustainable long-term monitoring capabilities [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0005), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0010). Extensive research in this field has demonstrated the versatility of SSCSs through various shapes and mix designs, enabling the measurement of critical parameters including strain, stress, and deflection [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0015), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0020), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0025). From a technical perspective, SSCSs provide significant durability advantages. Their lifespan matches that of the concrete structure itself, ensuring continuous monitoring capability throughout the service life without requiring frequent replacements or maintenance [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0030), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0035). Moreover, SSCSs enhance the mechanical properties of the concrete, resulting in improved overall durability of the structure [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0040).

The practical benefits of SSCSs extend to both economic and construction considerations. Compared to conventional SHM sensors, SSCSs offer substantial cost advantages, making them financially viable for large-scale infrastructure projects. Their flexibility during construction represents another crucial benefit, as they can be readily incorporated into various structural components while the concrete is in its fresh state [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0045), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0050). In terms of performance, SSCSs demonstrate exceptional monitoring capabilities through their high gauge factor, which indicates the sensor's sensitivity to variations in measured parameters. This enhanced sensitivity ensures precise and accurate measurements, enabling more effective SHM [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0055).

The combination of these advantages − durability, cost-effectiveness, construction flexibility, and high measurement sensitivity − establishes SSCSs as a promising technology for infrastructure monitoring. These characteristics collectively contribute to enhanced monitoring capabilities and improved maintenance strategies in structural engineering.

### 1.1. Introduction of SSCS

Smart self-sensing concrete sensor (SSCS) technology, initially proposed by an American researcher [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0070), has evolved into a promising field that continues to attract significant research attention. Modern studies have demonstrated SSCS's remarkable sensitivity to structural changes, including crack formation and external load application, establishing its effectiveness as a strain/stress monitoring system [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0025), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0060), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0065). The fundamental composition of SSCS involves the integration of specialized functional fillers into concrete ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#f0005)a). These fillers include various carbon-based materials such as nano carbon tubes (CNT), carbon black (CB), carbon fibres (CF), and carbon nano fibre (CNF), as well as other conductive materials like nickel powder and steel fibres [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0075), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0080). The incorporation of these materials transforms ordinary concrete into a self-sensing composite through carefully controlled dispersion processes, including mechanical mixing, chemical surfactant application, and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0085). The operating principle of SSCS is based on the piezoresistive effect of the integrated fillers within the cement-based matrix. When properly dispersed, these conductive materials create pathways for electron movement throughout the concrete matrix. These pathways exhibit high sensitivity to external loads, with their configuration changing in response to applied forces ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#f0005)b) [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0090). This mechanism enables the concrete to function as a sensor, translating mechanical deformation into measurable electrical changes. For practical implementation, the sensor's response is quantified by measuring its electrical resistivity (ER). This measurement is then calibrated against standard strain gauge readings, enabling the SSCS to function effectively as a strain sensor [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0085).

![Image 11](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S026322412402428X-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (467KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S026322412402428X-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (467KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S026322412402428X-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. (a) Materials used for SSCS manufacturing [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0070), and (b) a pathway provided by CNT in SSCS [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0090).

### 1.2. Structural response of bridges

Bridges represent critical components of modern infrastructure, serving as essential links in global transportation networks. Concrete bridges, in particular, have become prevalent due to their strength, durability, and versatility. These structures manifest in various forms, including beam bridges, arch bridges, cable-stayed bridges, and suspension bridges, each designed to meet specific transportation needs [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0095). The dynamic behaviour of concrete bridges presents a complex engineering challenge that demands careful consideration [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0100). Dynamic loading sources are diverse, encompassing vehicular traffic, wind forces, seismic activity, and pedestrian movement [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0105). A bridge's response to these dynamic forces is governed by multiple factors, including its natural frequency, damping characteristics, and the frequency and magnitude of the applied loads [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0110). When subjected to such dynamic forces, bridges exhibit various structural responses, including vertical and lateral vibrations, torsional movements, and potentially resonance effects [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0115), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0120). These responses significantly influence the long-term performance of both road and railway bridge structures [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0125), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0130).

Monitoring and assessing bridge behaviour requires measurement of key parameters, primarily strain, displacement, and acceleration [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0135). Traditional monitoring approaches typically employ strain gauges for measuring structural deformations. More recently, advanced technologies such as Fibre Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors have gained prominence, offering the capability to measure strains over extended distances with high precision [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0140). However, the measurement of strains in concrete bridges under dynamic loading presents significant challenges. The diverse nature of concrete and its vulnerability to cracking can create localized strain concentrations that may avoid detection by discretely placed sensors [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0145). As illustrated in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#t0005), various conventional sensors have been employed to monitor dynamic responses across different bridge types, including cable-stayed, concrete slab deck, steel structure, and steel arch bridges. These monitoring systems focus primarily on measuring strain, acceleration, and deflection parameters to assess structural behaviour under dynamic conditions.

Table 1. Summary of bridge dynamic response monitoring techniques.

| Ref. | Material | Structural type | Dynamic response | Sensors | Monitoring duration |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Huang et al. [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0150) | Concrete | Cable-stayed | Strain | 42 temperature sensors, and 24 strain sensors | 2 Months |
| Ghahremani et al. [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0155) | Concrete | Pretensioned adjacent box beams | Strain | 30 wireless strain gauges | Over 12 months |
| Saidin et al. [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0160) | Concrete | Slab | Acceleration | 15 accelerometers | Two phases (6 months apart) |
| Armijo and Zamora-Sánchez [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0165) | Steel-Concrete | A single isostatic span, girders with top slab | Acceleration, strain, deflection | MEMS wireless accelerometers | Over 12 months |
| Anastasopoulos and Reynders [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0170) | Steel | Riveted spans, I-shaped girders, I-shaped transverse beams, gravel ballast | Strain (long-term monitoring), acceleration (modal analysis) | 80 FBGs (Fibre Bragg Gratings), 4 thermocouples, and 15 wireless accelerometers | 1 Year |
| Bayane et al. [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0175) | Steel | Truss | Acceleration, strain, deflection (inclinometer) | 16 strain gauges, 5 piezoelectric uniaxial accelerometers, 1 inclinometer | 16 Months |
| Nguyen et al. [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0180) | Steel | Arch | Acceleration, strain | 16 bidirectional accelerometers (8 per span), 1 IEPE per span (Integrated Electronics Piezo-Electric) | 9 Months |
| ARTC [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0185) | Steel | Truss | Modal acquisition | Accelerometer and strain gauge | _ |

### 1.3. Bridge health monitoring systems

Bridge Health Monitoring Systems (BHMS) have emerged as essential tools for maintaining infrastructure safety and longevity [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0190). These sophisticated systems combine sensor networks with advanced data analysis capabilities to provide continuous assessment of structural integrity, enabling damage detection, maintenance forecasting, and enhanced safety management. Modern BHMS employ a comprehensive array of monitoring technologies. Traditional sensor systems include strain gauges, accelerometers, displacement sensors, and inclinometers for measuring local structural responses [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0195). Advanced technologies such as fibre optic sensors offer distributed strain and temperature measurements, while specialized corrosion sensors monitor degradation in reinforced concrete structures [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0200). For large-scale bridges, GPS sensors provide crucial data on global displacements. Recent technological advances have made these monitoring solutions increasingly robust and cost-effective [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0205).

However, the implementation of BHMS faces significant operational challenges. A primary concern is maintaining sensor reliability in harsh environmental conditions, particularly over extended periods. The management and processing of large data volumes present additional complexities, requiring sophisticated analysis systems and storage solutions. Power supply consistency remains problematic, especially for sensors deployed in remote locations. As detailed in [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#t0010), current monitoring methods exhibit specific limitations based on sensor type and application. Remote monitoring approaches, such as UAV-based systems, require specialized operators, favorable weather conditions, and complex post-processing of imagery data. More broadly, existing bridge monitoring methods can produce unreliable results through false or inaccurate readings, overestimated predictions, limited parameter recording capabilities, high sensitivity to environmental conditions, and variable response to different loading patterns.

Table 2. Comparative analysis of recent bridge monitoring techniques and their limitations.

| Ref. | Monitoring type | Output parameter | Material | Structural type | Limitations |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Contact | Contactless |
| Ayele et al. [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0210) |  | ✓ | Geometric parameters of cracks | Concrete | Prestressed | Requires specialized equipment (UAV, cameras), dependent on weather conditions for flights, needs expertise in image processing and deep learning, large datasets required for model training |
| Marchewka et al. [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0215) |  | ✓ | Rivet displacement/ position changes | Steel | Truss | Sensitive to image rotation, cannot detect subsurface corrosion, requires good lighting conditions |
| Corbally and Malekjafarian [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0220) | ✓ |  | Acceleration signals measured on the vehicle | Steel | Deck plate, with beams | Overestimates cracking damage, reduced accuracy in locating some types of damage when multiple damage types are considered simultaneously, relies on accuracy of FE model and damage simulation |
| Svendsen et al. [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0225) | ✓ |  | Acceleration measurements | Steel | Truss | Requires dense sensor network, does not account for environmental variability, high dimensionality of feature space limits features |
| Giordano et al. [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0230) |  | ✓ | Displacements (vertical and horizontal) | Steel | Truss deck | Limited to slow movements or sudden permanent deformations, cannot detect high-frequency vibrations, affected by atmospheric conditions |
| Hajializadeh [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0235) | ✓ |  | Accelerations measured on the passing train | Steel | Asymmetrical I-beam | Limited to laboratory conditions, requires instrumented vehicle, may be sensitive to environmental and operational variations, high computational requirements |
| Ardani et al. [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0240) | ✓ |  | Strain time histories | Steel | Truss | Requires significant computational resources for Transfer Learning, accuracy depends on damage intensity and location, performance affected by model uncertainties |
| Lan et al. [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0245) | ✓ |  | Raw vehicle acceleration signals | Steel | Simply supported beam | Limited to laboratory conditions, requires baseline data, may be sensitive to environmental factors |
| Li et al. [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0005) | ✓ |  | Scooter vibrations/ accelerations | Steel | Footbridge | Impractical sensor placement (attaching a smartphone to the front wheel of the scooter), limited environmental factors, supervised learning limitations, measurement accuracy |

### 1.4. Self-sensing concrete application in infrastructures monitoring

Self-sensing concrete composite technology has demonstrated versatility across diverse structural monitoring applications [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0250), [[51]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0255). These applications can be categorized into two distinct monitoring approaches based on their measurement objectives: The first category focuses on structural self-monitoring, where the self-sensing concrete measures responses originating from the structure itself. This includes detecting and measuring internal structural changes, deformations, and stress distributions within the concrete element. The second category encompasses adjacent response monitoring, where the self-sensing concrete functions as a sensor to detect and measure external influences. This includes applications such as weighing vehicles in motion and monitoring pedestrian movement patterns, where the sensor responds to loads and movements occurring in its vicinity but external to its own structure. This dual capability of self-sensing concrete composites – monitoring both internal structural responses and external loading effects – makes them particularly valuable for comprehensive infrastructure monitoring applications.

#### 1.4.1. Railway components

Bridges and railway infrastructure face continuous exposure to dynamic loads from vehicular and train traffic, making them ideal candidates for advanced monitoring systems. Self-sensing concrete sensors (SSCSs), being concrete-based materials, have demonstrated particular effectiveness in railway infrastructure monitoring, where their integration with existing concrete components facilitates both installation and data collection. This application has been recognized as a sustainable monitoring approach by researchers such as Jing et al. [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0045). In terms of material composition, carbon nanotubes (CNT) have emerged as a predominant nano filler choice for railway applications, as evidenced by extensive research documented in [Table 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#t0015). The monitoring capabilities of these sensors rely on measurements of ER and fractional change of resistivity (FCR). These measurements enable the detection and assessment of various railway-specific parameters, including ballast condition assessment, dynamic response monitoring of sleepers, and crack initiation detection. Researchers have successfully implemented SSCSs in various concrete components of railway tracks, particularly in sleepers and slabs. The construction of these sensors has been achieved through two primary dispersion methods: ultrasonic sonication and Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD). The latter technique involves growing carbon directly on cement particles [[52]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026322412402428X#b0260). These implementation approaches have been validated through comparison with conventional sensors, with additional studies examining the influence of environmental factors such as moisture on sensor performance.

Table 3. Application of SSCS in railway infrastructure monitoring.

FULL CONTENT:
Contested Logistics and Sustainment, ASA(ALT) | xTech, Phase I

Bridge Health Monitoring System

Release Date: 02/05/2025

Solicitation: 25.4

Open Date: 07/09/2025

Topic Number: A254-017

Application Due Date: 07/09/2025

Duration: Up to 6 months

Close Date: 07/09/2025

Amount Up To: $250,000

We are no longer accepting white papers. SBIR submissions are now limited to those eligible through the xTech competition.
IMPORTANT: xTechIgnite will be used to identify small business concerns that meet the criteria for award. Winners selected from the xTechIgnite prize competition will be the only firms eligible to submit an SBIR proposal under any of the topics listed above. Proposals submitted to the topics listed above by nonwinners of the xTechIgnite competition will not be evaluated. See the full xTechIgnite competition RFI here: https://www.xtech.army.mil/competition/xtechignite/.  
The white paper submission deadline for xTechIgnite is March 12, 2025. White papers must be submitted by following instructions provided at the xTechIgnite link above. NOTE: white papers are NOT submitted to DSIP. Small business concerns that do not submit a concept white paper to the xTechIgnite competition before the March 12, 2025 deadline will be ineligible to compete or submit a full SBIR proposal to DSIP.  
Objective
The current methods for assessing military bridges rely on Preventative Maintenance Check and Services (PMCS) combined with fatigue monitoring crack gauges, which have proven insufficient for accurately determining a bridge’s remaining service life. This inaccuracy forces the Army to maintain a surplus of bridges to mitigate the risk of unexpected failures. Research highlights that vehicle crossings, rather than environmental factors, are the primary determinants of bridge health, with the impact varying significantly by vehicle type.
To address this, the Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) system seeks to revolutionize bridge assessment and management through an integrated approach combining advanced sensor technology, onboard data storage, and adaptable communication capabilities. By continuously monitoring and recording vehicle traffic, structural stress, and environmental conditions, the BHM system will enable precise predictions of a bridge’s remaining service life. This innovation promises to reduce reliance on manual inspections, enhance situational awareness for battlefield commanders, and optimize bridge asset utilization, ensuring military readiness and operational efficiency.
Description
The Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) project will develop a robust, modular, and scalable technology suite for real-time monitoring and assessment of military and commercial bridge health. This integrated system will empower battlefield commanders and sustainers by providing actionable insights into bridge readiness and lifespan. The proposed solution should address a combination of advanced sensors, onboard storage systems, and secure communication technologies to achieve the following objectives:
1. Types of Bridges

The BHM system must prioritize adaptability to a variety of bridge types. These include:
Portable All-Metal Bridges: Commonly used in military operations, these structures require rapid deployment capabilities and sensors that can withstand frequent assembly, disassembly, and storage.
Composite Bridges: Combining concrete and metal, these structures are often used for their durability and lightweight properties.
Reinforced Concrete Bridges: Found in both military and civilian applications, these bridges are vulnerable to environmental factors such as corrosion and require monitoring for cracks and stress.
Suspension and Cable-Stayed Bridges: Complex commercial structures where cable tension and tower integrity are critical monitoring points.
Precast Modular Bridges: Often used for temporary or emergency infrastructure, these bridges benefit from scalable monitoring solutions for varying sizes and configurations.

2. Real-time and Periodic Monitoring
The BHM system will monitor key indicators of structural integrity, including stress, fatigue, and damage, through a modular sensor suite. While real-time communication may be beneficial for specific use cases (e.g., commercial highway bridges), the primary focus is on:

Onboard Storage: Capturing and securely storing critical data directly on the bridge.
Periodic Data Retrieval: Allowing personnel, vehicles, or UAVs to retrieve stored data as needed, minimizing security risks and operational costs associated with trackable continuous transmissions.
Future Adaptability: Ensuring the system design allows for the potential of wireless communication integration in future phases if operational needs evolve.

3. Predictive Analytics and Storage Monitoring
In addition to operational bridges, the BHM system must maintain information on the health of portable bridges during storage. This capability ensures readiness for deployment. Predictive analytics should incorporate:

Usage data, including crossings and load impacts, are recorded before storage.
Modular components enable cost-effective monitoring and allow reusable components to be deployed elsewhere once the portable bridge is stored.

4. Indefinite Data Storage and Management
The system must include a robust mechanism for the indefinite storage of bridge health data. This includes maintaining detailed historical records of usage, environmental exposure, and maintenance activities. The solution should employ secure, scalable storage systems, ensuring data integrity and accessibility for lifecycle management and continuous improvement.
5. Sensor Suite Requirements
The sensor package must be:

Modular: Adaptable to different bridge designs and operational needs.
Scalable: Capable of monitoring bridges of various sizes, materials, and load capacities.
Durable: Resistant to environmental stresses such as extreme temperatures, corrosion, and vibration.
Comprehensive: Equipped with advanced tools, such as strain gauges, ultrasonic sensors, fiber-optic monitoring systems, RFID readers, and AI-powered traffic classification capabilities.
Focused on Storage Data: Ensuring essential data components and other non-removable components remain with the bridge while advanced systems (e.g. communications, power) can be redeployed to other bridges still in use.

6. Energy Efficiency and Environmental Resilience
The solution must function autonomously with portable power sources for mobile bridges and integrate with fixed power systems for stationary bridges. Sensors must operate reliably in diverse and challenging environments, from combat zones to urban areas.
7. Secure, Adaptable Data Communication
Data collected by the system will be securely transmitted when necessary. For initial feasibility, the focus is on:

Localized, onboard data storage with retrieval options for personnel or vehicles.
Secure communication mechanisms for direct connection, selective transmission, including burst transmissions or UAV retrieval.
Flexibility for future enhancements to support continuous or real-time monitoring if required.

IMPORTANT: A prize competition, xTechIgnite, will be used to identify small business concerns that meet the criteria for award for this topic. Winners selected from the xTechIgnite prize competition will be the only firms eligible to submit a SBIR proposal under this topic. All other proposals will not be evaluated. See the full xTechIgnite competition details here: https://www.xtech.army.mil/competition/xtechignite/. 
Phase I
This topic is only accepting Phase I proposals for a cost up to $250,000 for a 6-month period of performance.
Phase I of the Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) project will culminate in a comprehensive report that explores the various aspects of the system. The report will provide an in-depth analysis of the sensor technologies evaluated for potential use in the solution, including the selected sensors and the rationale behind their choice.
The study will also delve into the critical aspects of bridge health communication, power supply, and data management. Specifically, it will address how bridge health information will be transmitted to Commanders and Maintainers in an electronically monitored environment, ensuring seamless integration and situational awareness without undue risk. Additionally, the report will outline the power supply requirements for the BHM system and propose a solution for maintaining bridge health data continuity, regardless of whether the bridge is deployed or stored in a facility.
While the primary focus of the SBIR is on developing a BHM solution for the Heavy Assault Scissor Bridge (HASB), the Phase I investigation will also examine the potential for commonality across Army Bridge systems. The study will identify the essential components that must remain with a bridge and those that could be removed and adapted for use on other bridge types, thereby promoting a level of standardization and interoperability across the Army’s bridge inventory.
The Phase I report will provide a thorough understanding of the BHM system’s requirements, capabilities, and potential for scalability, laying the foundation for the development of a robust and effective bridge health monitoring solution.
Phase II
Phase II of the Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) project will focus on the fabrication and testing of a prototype system, building on the research and analysis conducted in Phase I. A key aspect of this phase will be the implementation of robust cyber security measures to ensure the system’s integrity and protect against potential threats.
In addition to prototype development, the vendor will conduct a comprehensive sustainability assessment of the selected components to guarantee a viable path for long-term support and maintenance. This assessment will ensure that the chosen technical solutions can be supported and upgraded over time, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and ensuring the system’s continued effectiveness.
The prototype development process will involve the creation of off-bridge components and breadboard systems, which will be thoroughly tested and optimized to ensure seamless integration and performance. The final prototype will be designed to be compatible with the Heavy Assault Scissor Bridge (HASB) and will include all necessary equipment to function as proposed.
In collaboration with the PdM Bridging team and the GVSC Bridge Technology Lab, the prototype will undergo rigorous validation and testing on a HASB to ensure its performance and accuracy. The vendor will also conduct a live demonstration of the solution at Fort Leonard Wood, showcasing its capabilities on a local bridge.
The deliverables for Phase II will include two complete systems, which will support additional field evaluations and testing. These systems will provide the Army with a tangible, functional solution for monitoring bridge health, enabling more informed decision-making and improved maintenance practices.
Phase III

Critical Infrastructure: This full stack option can be fitted to provide the same real-time, non-invasive analysis. ​
Predictive Maintenance: Can provide real-time feedback on the performance of a vehicle, equipment part, etc., to save time on repairs. ​
Smart Cities: Allows for various sensors to track various assets at once in one single “pane of glass.”

Submission Information
For more information, and to submit your full proposal package, visit the DSIP Portal.

URL: https://www.interfaceforce.com/how-sensor-technologies-help-build-better-highways-bridges-and-roadways/

SUMMARY:
Interface load cells, load pins, instrumentation, multi-axis sensors, and torque transducers are top choices for those engaged in highway, roadway, and bridge

FULL CONTENT:
![Interface]()

#### Industry Solutions

#### Product Applications

#### Interface Guides

#### Custom Solutions

#### Company Overview

![]()

# [How Sensor Technologies Help Build Better Highways, Bridges, and Roadways](https://www.interfaceforce.com/how-sensor-technologies-help-build-better-highways-bridges-and-roadways/ "Permanent Link: How Sensor Technologies Help Build Better Highways, Bridges, and Roadways")

![]()The global [infrastructure sector](https://www.interfaceforce.com/literature/infrastructure-brochure/) is booming, with massive investments in roads, highways, bridges, and transportation networks. This growth increases the demand for accurate and reliable force and torque measurement solutions.

![]()

Sensor technologies are in high demand for transportation infrastructure. Driven by increasing [industrial automation](https://www.interfaceforce.com/solutions/industrial-automation/), rising demand for precision measurement, and stringent safety regulations, force, torque, and weighing solutions are necessary for ensuring safety, optimizing performance, and managing costs throughout the infrastructure lifecycle. Civil engineers and manufacturers turn to Interface for accurate, quality measurement solutions, from construction and equipment operation to ongoing maintenance and monitoring.

### Highway, Road, and Bridge Construction, Equipment and Monitoring

Interface **load cells, load pins, instrumentation, multi-axis sensors,** and **torque transducers** are top choices for those engaged in highway, roadway, and bridge infrastructure projects and testing. Sensors commonly measure the hardware used for transportation infrastructure products and structures.

**Construction** projects and equipment utilize Interface products. The global highway construction market is expected to reach $848.3 billion by 2027 (Source: Global Market Insights), which indicates a significant need for force and torque measurement in road construction equipment and ongoing pavement monitoring. Our load cells monitor loads, prevent overloads, and ensure safe operation in cranes, pile drivers, and other heavy machinery.

For example, Interface’s high-capacity load cells are vital for weighing bridge sections during installation. Load pins are integrated into lifting attachments and rigging systems to measure tension and ensure stability during construction. Torque transducers monitor the tightening of bolts in critical connections, ensuring structural integrity and preventing failures, which is essential for bridge construction and securing tunnel linings. Read: [How Load Cells Are Transforming the Construction Industry](https://www.interfaceforce.com/how-load-cells-are-transforming-the-construction-industry/ "How Load Cells Are Transforming the Construction Industry")

**Equipment** utilizes force sensors embedded in the machinery, including excavators and bulldozers, to optimize performance, reduce wear and tear, and improve fuel efficiency. Real-time data on digging forces and ground conditions can help operators work more effectively. Read: [Interface Solutions for Heavy Equipment](https://www.interfaceforce.com/interface-solutions-for-heavy-equipment/ "Interface Solutions for Heavy Equipment")

### Use Cases and Applications

**Transportation Tunnel Boring**

A tunnel boring machine, or TBM, excavates tunnels for highways and roadways. TBMs are designed to cut through rock and soil with a rotating cutter head. The torque of the cutting wheel needs to be monitored and tested to ensure it works correctly during excavation operations. Interface’s [T2 Ultra Precision Shaft Style Rotary Torque Transducers](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/torque-transducers/rotary/t2-ultra-precision-shaft-style-rotary-torque-transducer/) are attached to the cutter drives of the cutting wheel used to cut through the material. When connected to the [BX8-AS BlueDAQ Series Data Acquisition System](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/digital-instrumentation/signal-conditioners-digital-instrumentation/bx8-as-bluedaq-series-data-acquisition-system-with-industrial-enclosure/), the customer can monitor the torque of the TBM cutting wheel mechanism when connected to the computer with the supplied BlueDAQ software.

**Wind Monitoring During Bridge Construction**

Wind monitoring is necessary during bridge construction. Strong winds can destroy a bridge under construction, a work in progress with poor structural design. Monitoring these winds in real time is much more accurate than predicting weather forecasts. Interface suggests installing the [WTS-WSS Wireless Wind Speed Transmitter Module](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/instrumentation/wts-wss-wireless-wind-speed-transmitter-module/) on the highest point of construction, such as a crane. Wind speed results are wirelessly transmitted to the customer’s computer through the [WTS-BS-4 Wireless Base Station with a USB in the Industrial Enclosure](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/wireless/base-stations/wts-bs-4-wireless-base-station-with-usb-interface-in-industrial-enclosure/). They can also be transmitted to the [WTS-BS-1 Wireless Handheld Display for Unlimited Transmitters](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/wireless/base-stations/wts-bs-1-wireless-handheld-display-for-unlimited-transmitters/). Data can be displayed, logged, and graphed with the supplied Log100 software. Review the [application note here](https://www.interfaceforce.com/solutions/infrastructure/bridge-construction-wind-monitoring/).

**Bridge Seismic Monitoring**

The customer would like to monitor seismic activity on a bridge by using force sensors and continuously monitoring bridge forces before, during, and after earthquakes occur. The customer would prefer a wireless solution so they would not need to run long cables on the bridge. Using [Interface’s Wireless LP Load Pin](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/load-pins-tension-load-links-and-load-shackles/load-pins/wtslp-wireless-custom-stainless-steel-load-pin/), customized to fit their needs, LP Load Pins and the [WTS-AM-1E Acquisition Module](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/wireless/acquisition-modules/wts-am-1e-wireless-strain-bridge-transmitter-module/) are installed onto the bridge.  [WTS-BS-4 Industrial Base Station](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/wireless/base-stations/wts-bs-4-wireless-base-station-with-usb-interface-in-industrial-enclosure/) is connected to a computer and installed up to 800 meters from the WTS-AM-1E Acquisition Module. The computers are also connected to a Solar Panel Backup System to ensure continuous operation during power outages. [Read more here.](https://www.interfaceforce.com/solutions/infrastructure/bridge-seismic-force-monitoring-solution/)

**Road Bridge Lift Monitoring**

Road bridge lifts are specially constructed on roads or highways that intersect with water bodies such as rivers, canals, or harbors, allowing navigation to continue unimpeded. A force sensor system is needed to ensure the bridge’s structural integrity and safe operation. Interface suggests installing  [LP Stainless Steel Load Pins](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/load-pins-tension-load-links-and-load-shackles/load-pins/lp-custom-stainless-steel-load-pin/) within the pulleys. The load pins will detect the force implemented when the bridge is lifted. Results are displayed for the customer using the [920i Programmable Weight Indicator and Controller](https://www.interfaceforce.com/products/digital-instrumentation/signal-conditioners-digital-instrumentation/920i-programmable-weight-indicator-and-controller/). Read more [here.](https://www.interfaceforce.com/solutions/infrastructure/road-bridge-lift-monitoring/)

### Interface Measurement Solutions for Global Transporation Infrastructure

Interface’s commitment to quality, accuracy, and durability makes its solutions ideal for the demanding environment of transportation infrastructure projects, including highways, bridges, and roads. These projects are used worldwide to ensure the safety, efficiency, and longevity of critical infrastructure assets today and tomorrow. Interface sensor technologies and instrumentation are also integral to building smart transportation infrastructure. They enable real-time monitoring and data-driven decision-making and are helping to drive innovations in mobility structures, including green transportation networks.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent innovations include self-sensing concrete sensors (SSCS) capable of monitoring structural health in real-time, enhancing accuracy and sustainability in bridge assessments.
AI algorithms analyze sensor data to predict structural issues, enabling proactive maintenance and improving decision-making processes for bridge safety management.

enriched_points.point_title: Potential of blockchain for data security
enriched_points.expanded_content: Blockchain technology can provide secure, immutable records of bridge health data, enhancing transparency and trust in monitoring systems while protecting against unauthorized access.
